events.title: Grant Writing for First-Time Applicants (Online Webinar)
events.description: A practical online webinar for researchers preparing their first grant or fellowship application. The session will cover how to read a funding call, structure a strong proposal, and avoid common mistakes.

events.title: National AI Funding Programs Briefing
events.description: An online briefing on current and upcoming national programs supporting Artificial Intelligence research, including eligibility, funding instruments, and evaluation criteria.

events.title: Program Officer Clinic: One-to-One Consultations
events.description: Book a 20-minute slot with a program officer to discuss your project idea, fit with available calls, and questions about the application process.
events.location: National Research Council, Room 3A, Berlin (with optional video call slots)

events.title: Designing Impact Evaluation in Research Projects
events.description: An in-person workshop focusing on how to plan and evidence impact in research funding applications, including logic models and theory of change exercises.
events.topic: Impact evaluation and theory of change

events.title: Climate Data Fellowship Call 2025 – Information Day
events.description: An information day covering the objectives, eligibility, budget rules, and evaluation process for the 2025 Climate Data Fellowship call.

events.title: Grant Writing Intensive for Early-Career Researchers
events.description: A full-day, hands-on workshop where participants will draft key sections of their next grant application and receive peer and facilitator feedback.
events.location: National Research Council Training Centre, Berlin

events.title: Building a Realistic Project Budget
events.description: Online webinar guiding applicants through eligible cost categories, budget justification, and common pitfalls in project budgeting.

events.title: Environment & Climate Calls 2025–2026: Overview Session
events.description: A session presenting the range of Environment & Climate funding programs, including thematic priorities and indicative budgets.

events.title: Sustainable Cities Initiative – Program Launch
events.description: Launch event for the Sustainable Cities Initiative funding program, including presentations of objectives, budget, and example project concepts.

events.title: Information Session for Early-Career Investigator Grants
events.description: Online information session focusing on early-career eligibility, career stage definitions, and mentoring requirements in individual research grants.

events.title: Roundtable on National AI Programs and Ethics
events.description: A hybrid roundtable bringing together program officers and researchers to discuss ethical considerations in national AI funding programs.
events.location: National Research Council Auditorium, Berlin (with livestream)

events.title: Data Management Plan Clinic
events.description: Hands-on clinic supporting applicants in drafting data management plans that comply with open data and FAIR requirements.

events.title: Research Ethics and Integrity for Grant Applicants
events.description: Online training session covering ethical review requirements, integrity statements, and responsible conduct of research in funding applications.

events.title: Open Access and Publication Requirements in Funded Projects
events.description: Webinar explaining open access mandates, acceptable publication venues, and budgeting for article processing charges in project proposals.

helparticles.title: Who can be a Principal Investigator?
helparticles.content:
To act as a Principal Investigator (PI), you must hold a contract or appointment at an eligible institution for the full duration of the proposed project. In most cases, this means being employed as a faculty member, senior researcher, or equivalent role with at least a 50% appointment. Postdoctoral researchers may serve as PI if their contract covers the full project period or the host institution provides a written commitment to extend the contract if funding is awarded.

PhD candidates and students are generally **not** eligible to act as PI, but they may be listed as team members or co-investigators where permitted by the specific call. Some early-career schemes allow near-completion PhD candidates to apply, provided that the degree is formally awarded before the project start date; such exceptions are always stated explicitly in the call text.

The final decision on PI eligibility is made at the time of administrative screening. If you are unsure whether your role qualifies, contact your research office or the program officer for clarification before submitting an application.

helparticles.title: Can PhD students apply as Principal Investigator?
helparticles.content:
PhD students are usually **not eligible** to act as Principal Investigators (PIs) under our funding schemes. Standard calls require the PI to hold an employment contract or equivalent status that extends at least until the expected project end date. PhD students typically do not meet this requirement.

However, PhD candidates may participate as team members, research assistants, or co-authors of the proposal. Some early-career fellowship calls allow applications from advanced PhD candidates who will obtain their degree before the project start date; in those cases, the call text specifies whether a PhD student can be named as PI and under which conditions.

If you are a PhD student considering a leadership role, we recommend that you (1) confirm with your supervisor or department whether they can act as PI on your behalf, and (2) review the section "Who can be a Principal Investigator?". For call-specific exceptions, please consult the call guidelines or contact the program officer.

helparticles.title: How are career stages (early, mid, senior) defined?
helparticles.content:
Career stages are generally defined with reference to the date on which you were awarded your PhD (or equivalent doctoral degree). Unless otherwise specified in the call, our standard definitions are:

- Early-career: 0–7 years post-PhD at the call deadline
- Mid-career: 7–15 years post-PhD at the call deadline
- Senior: more than 15 years post-PhD at the call deadline

Eligible career breaks (e.g. parental leave, long-term illness, national service) may be deducted from the post-PhD time when calculating your career stage. Each call may specify additional rules or narrower ranges. Always check the call text under "Applicant eligibility" for call-specific definitions.

If your situation is complex (e.g. multiple doctorates, non-linear career paths), you should describe it briefly in the CV or eligibility section and, if needed, contact the program officer for guidance.

helparticles.title: Which institutions are eligible to host a project?
helparticles.content:
Eligible host institutions typically include universities, higher education institutions, public research organizations, and certain non-profit organizations with a significant research mandate. Private companies are generally not eligible to host projects under academic research calls, but may participate as partners or subcontractors where indicated.

The host institution must be legally established in an eligible country, have the administrative capacity to manage public funds, and agree to the program’s terms and conditions. For some calls, only institutions within specified regions or priority areas may apply.

If you are unsure whether your organization qualifies, consult your research support office or send a brief description of your institution to the program officer before starting an application.

helparticles.title: Can I resubmit a previously unsuccessful proposal?
helparticles.content:
Many programs allow resubmission of previously unsuccessful proposals, provided that you have substantially revised the project in line with reviewer feedback. Some calls impose a waiting period or limit the number of times a proposal (or PI) can be submitted.

Where resubmissions are allowed, you should explicitly indicate this in the application form and, if applicable, describe how the new version differs from the earlier one. If a call does not mention resubmissions, they are generally permitted, but the program may apply internal checks to prevent duplicate submissions within the same deadline.

Always consult the call text under "Restrictions on resubmission" and contact the program officer if you are unsure.

helparticles.title: How do I start a new application?
helparticles.content:
To start a new application, first locate a relevant funding call under the "Find funding" section. Open the call details page and review the eligibility and budget information. When you are ready, click the button labeled "Apply now" or "Start new application".

You will be taken to the first step of the online application form, where you can enter the project title, summary, start date, duration, and requested budget. Your progress is saved when you click "Save as draft" or when you explicitly move to the next section. You can return to your applications at any time via the "My applications" page.

helparticles.title: Can I save my application as a draft and return later?
helparticles.content:
Yes. You can save your application as a draft at any point before submission. Use the "Save as draft" button at the bottom of the page or the "Save and continue" button when moving between sections. Saving as draft does not submit your application to the funding body.

All draft applications are listed on the "My applications" page, where you can reopen them for editing. We recommend saving frequently, especially before uploading attachments or making significant changes. Drafts are retained for at least 18 months after the call deadline, unless otherwise stated.

helparticles.title: How do I submit my application?
helparticles.content:
Once you have completed all required sections of the application form and uploaded the necessary documents, navigate to the "Review & submit" section. The system will highlight any missing mandatory fields or validation errors.

When all checks are passed, you can click the "Submit application" button. You will receive an on-screen confirmation and an email containing your application ID and submission timestamp. After submission, only minor administrative details (such as contact information) can be changed by contacting the program officer; the core scientific content cannot be edited.

helparticles.title: Which cost categories are eligible?
helparticles.content:
Eligible costs vary by program, but typically include personnel (salaries and related costs), equipment, travel and subsistence, consumables, publication and dissemination costs, and certain indirect or overhead costs. All requested costs must be directly related to the project and incurred during the funded period.

Ineligible costs commonly include general office equipment, standard IT infrastructure not specific to the project, debt servicing, and costs incurred before the project start or after the project end. Each call provides a detailed list of eligible and ineligible costs; in case of doubt, consult the call guidelines or contact the program officer.

helparticles.title: Are travel costs eligible and is there a maximum travel budget?
helparticles.content:
Travel costs that are directly related to the project (for example, fieldwork, collaboration meetings, or presenting results at conferences) are generally eligible. Standard practice is to follow the host institution’s travel policy regarding class of travel, daily allowances, and accommodation limits.

Some calls specify a maximum percentage or absolute cap for travel costs relative to the total project budget. If a call includes such a cap, it will be described in the "Budget rules" or "Eligible costs" section. International conference travel is typically allowed, but may be subject to justification requirements.

If you are planning a budget with significant travel components, check the call text carefully and, where necessary, contact the program officer to confirm any maximum travel budget or specific restrictions.

helparticles.title: How should I calculate indirect costs or overheads?
helparticles.content:
Indirect costs (also referred to as overheads) are intended to cover general institutional support costs that cannot be directly attributed to a single project. Many programs apply a fixed indirect cost rate, such as a percentage of eligible direct costs (excluding subcontracts).

Where a fixed rate is specified, you should not exceed it in your budget. If your institution has a lower internal rate, you may use that rate instead, but the difference cannot be reallocated to other cost categories. In all cases, indirect costs must be calculated in accordance with the rules of the specific funding program and your institution’s usual accounting practices.

helparticles.title: Which browsers are supported by the application system?
helparticles.content:
The online application system is tested with the latest two major versions of Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and Safari on desktop operating systems. Older browser versions or mobile browsers may not be fully supported and can lead to display or upload issues.

For the best experience, we recommend using an up-to-date desktop browser and ensuring that JavaScript and cookies are enabled. If you encounter technical problems, try clearing your cache, disabling browser extensions, or switching to another supported browser before contacting technical support.

helparticles.title: How can I create or edit funding alerts?
helparticles.content:
To create or edit funding alerts, go to "My profile" and select the "Funding alerts" section. Click "Create new alert" to define keywords, research areas, geographic scope, notification frequency, and deadline preferences.

You can edit or deactivate an existing alert at any time by selecting it from the list. Changes take effect immediately, but delivery of email summaries depends on the chosen frequency (e.g. daily, weekly, or monthly summaries).

helparticles.title: What is "My saved help" and how do I use it?
helparticles.content:
"My saved help" is a personalized list of help center and FAQ articles that you have bookmarked for quick access. On any article page, you can click the "Save article" or "Add to my saved help" button to add it to this list.

You can access all saved articles via the "My saved help" link in the footer or from your profile area. Articles remain in your saved list until you remove them, even if they are updated by the editorial team.

helpcategories.name: Eligibility
helpcategories.description: Eligibility rules for applicants, institutions, and project types, including who can act as Principal Investigator.

helpcategories.description: Guidance on starting, editing, saving, and submitting online applications.

helpcategories.description: Information on eligible and ineligible costs, travel budgets, and indirect cost calculations.

helpcategories.description: Technical requirements and troubleshooting tips for using the online application system.

helpcategories.name: Account & notifications
helpcategories.description: Managing your user account, saved help articles, and funding alerts.

fundingprograms.title: Sustainable Cities Initiative
fundingprograms.description: A flagship program supporting interdisciplinary research and innovation projects that advance sustainable urban development, low-carbon infrastructure, and climate-resilient city planning.

fundingprograms.title: Environmental Challenge Grants
fundingprograms.description: Competitive grants for applied environmental research addressing biodiversity loss, water quality, pollution reduction, and ecosystem restoration at regional and national scales.
fundingprograms.hostOrganizationName: Ministry of Environment and Climate Action

fundingprograms.title: Climate Adaptation Pilot Projects
fundingprograms.description: Funding for pilot-scale projects that test and evaluate innovative climate adaptation measures, including nature-based solutions and community resilience strategies.

fundingprograms.title: Climate Data Fellowship Programme
fundingprograms.description: A fellowship program supporting individual researchers who develop and apply advanced data and modelling approaches to climate risk, forecasting, and resilience planning.

fundingprograms.title: Early-Career Investigator Research Grants
fundingprograms.description: Individual research grants for early-career researchers (0–7 years post-PhD) across all disciplines, supporting the establishment of independent research lines.

fundingprograms.title: National Artificial Intelligence Research Program
fundingprograms.description: A strategic program funding fundamental and applied AI research, including machine learning, trustworthy AI, and AI for science and industry.

fundingprograms.title: AI and Society Challenge Grants
fundingprograms.description: Interdisciplinary projects examining the social, ethical, and regulatory implications of Artificial Intelligence in democratic societies.
fundingprograms.hostOrganizationName: Council for Social and Technological Futures

fundingprograms.title: Translational Health Research Program
fundingprograms.description: Support for projects that translate basic biomedical discoveries into clinical applications, diagnostics, or interventions that improve patient outcomes.

fundingprograms.title: Social Innovation in Ageing Societies
fundingprograms.description: Funding for social science and mixed-methods projects addressing ageing populations, social inclusion, and innovative care models.

fundingprograms.title: Engineering Frontier Projects
fundingprograms.description: Large-scale, high-risk engineering projects developing transformative technologies in energy, materials, transport, and manufacturing.

fundingprograms.title: Open Discovery Grants
fundingprograms.description: Investigator-led basic research grants in any field of science, technology, engineering, and humanities, with light thematic restrictions.

fundingprograms.title: International Collaborative Climate Research Program
fundingprograms.description: Joint calls with international partner agencies to support collaborative climate science, mitigation, and adaptation research involving teams from multiple countries.

fundedprojects.title: Green Corridors for Climate-Resilient Cities
fundedprojects.description: Implementation and evaluation of interconnected green corridors to reduce urban heat, manage stormwater, and enhance biodiversity in three medium-sized cities.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Delivered evidence that integrated green corridors can reduce local ambient temperatures by up to 2�c and cut peak stormwater runoff by 18%. Results informed new municipal climate adaptation plans.

fundedprojects.title: Smart Mobility Living Labs for Low-Carbon Districts
fundedprojects.description: A network of living labs testing low-carbon mobility solutions, including demand-responsive transit, micromobility, and data-driven traffic management in dense urban districts.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Demonstrated CO2 emission reductions of up to 24% in pilot districts and produced open datasets and policy toolkits now used by several municipalities.

fundedprojects.title: Circular Construction Pilots in Urban Regeneration
fundedprojects.description: Pilot projects integrating circular construction principles into large-scale urban regeneration, focusing on material reuse, low-carbon building systems, and citizen engagement.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Mid-term results show a 35% reduction in construction waste on pilot sites and strong stakeholder interest in circular procurement guidelines.

fundedprojects.title: River Restoration Networks for Biodiversity and Flood Control
fundedprojects.description: Applied research and demonstration sites restoring river floodplains to improve biodiversity and reduce downstream flood risk in agricultural regions.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Restoration measures increased habitat diversity and reduced peak flood levels by up to 12 cm in test catchments, informing new river basin management plans.

fundedprojects.title: Urban Air Quality Sensor Networks for Policy Support
fundedprojects.description: Deployment of dense low-cost sensor networks to map intra-urban air quality patterns and evaluate the impact of traffic and heating policies.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Interim analyses reveal pollution hotspots close to schools and bus depots, leading to targeted mitigation measures by local authorities.

fundedprojects.title: Coastal Resilience Living Labs
fundedprojects.description: Pilot implementation of nature-based coastal protection measures combined with community engagement to test adaptation options against sea-level rise.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Living labs reduced erosion rates by up to 40% and increased local awareness of climate risks, providing scalable models for other coastal regions.

fundedprojects.title: Mountain Watershed Adaptation Strategies
fundedprojects.description: Testing integrated water management and ecosystem-based adaptation measures in snow-dependent mountain watersheds.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Early findings suggest that combined forest restoration and smart reservoir management can substantially improve drought resilience downstream.

fundedprojects.title: Machine Learning for Real-Time Flood Forecasting
fundedprojects.description: A Climate Data Fellowship project using deep learning and data assimilation techniques to improve short-term river flood forecasts.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Developed an open-source forecasting toolkit that reduced mean absolute error in flood peak predictions by 27% compared to baseline models.

fundedprojects.title: Data-Driven Analytics of Urban Heat Islands
fundedprojects.description: Fellowship project combining satellite data, in-situ measurements, and socio-economic datasets to map urban heat exposure and identify vulnerable communities.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Preliminary maps highlight strong inequalities in heat exposure, supporting targeted adaptation measures in low-income neighborhoods.

fundedprojects.title: Data-Driven Climate Governance in Mid-Sized Cities
fundedprojects.description: An early-career investigator project exploring how city governments can use climate and mobility data to design equitable mitigation policies.
fundedprojects.leadOrganizationName: School of Public Policy and Data Science, CityTech University
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Early outputs include open datasets and decision-support dashboards co-designed with three pilot municipalities.

fundedprojects.title: Trustworthy AI for Clinical Decision Support
fundedprojects.description: Developing interpretable machine learning models and validation frameworks for supporting clinicians in diagnosis and treatment decisions.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Interim results show improved predictive performance with interpretable models and active collaboration with two university hospitals.

fundedprojects.title: Algorithmic Fairness and Democratic Governance
fundedprojects.description: Interdisciplinary research on how algorithmic decision systems affect democratic institutions, with policy recommendations for accountable AI governance.
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Produced widely cited guidelines on algorithmic transparency and oversight now referenced in national AI strategies.

fundedprojects.title: Cross-Border River Basin Climate Adaptation
fundedprojects.description: International collaborative project aligning climate adaptation strategies in a transboundary river basin shared by three countries.
fundedprojects.leadOrganizationName: International Centre for Transboundary Water Cooperation
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Partners have agreed on a shared climate risk assessment framework and piloted harmonised flood early warning systems.

fundedprojects.title: Fundamental Studies in Atmospheric Wave Dynamics
fundedprojects.description: Investigator-led basic research on atmospheric wave propagation and its role in large-scale circulation and extreme weather events.
fundedprojects.leadOrganizationName: Department of Atmospheric Physics, State University
fundedprojects.outcomesSummary: Improved theoretical understanding of atmospheric wave-mean flow interactions, leading to refinements in global climate model parameterizations.

budgetlineitems.description: Principal Investigator (0.4 FTE) and research assistant (0.5 FTE) for 24 months
High-performance workstation and secure data storage for climate data processing
Conference travel and collaboration visits (adjusted to meet overall budget cap)
Postdoctoral researcher in machine learning for 18 months
Attendance at two international AI in medicine conferences

applications.title: Data-Driven Climate Resilience
applications.shortSummary: The project will integrate high-resolution climate, infrastructure, and socio-economic data to model local climate risks and support resilient urban planning decisions. It will develop open-source analytics tools and dashboards that city planners can use to test adaptation scenarios. The fellowship focuses on reproducible methods that can be transferred to other municipalities.

applications.title: Trustworthy AI for Clinical Decision Support
applications.shortSummary: This application proposes interpretable machine learning models to assist clinicians in diagnosis and treatment selection for complex cases. The project will combine clinical trial data, electronic health records, and expert feedback loops to ensure robustness and explainability. Ethical and regulatory compliance will be addressed through dedicated work packages on bias assessment and validation.

applications.title: Urban Heat Mitigation Pilot in Vulnerable Districts
applications.shortSummary: The project will test a package of nature-based and architectural measures to reduce extreme heat exposure in two low-income urban districts. Using microclimate modelling and community co-design workshops, the team will evaluate which combinations of interventions provide the highest health and comfort benefits. Lessons learned will feed into local adaptation plans and national guidance.

applications.title: Fundamental Studies in Atmospheric Wave Dynamics
applications.shortSummary: An investigator-led basic research project on the generation and propagation of atmospheric waves and their influence on large-scale circulation. The work will combine analytical theory, idealised numerical simulations, and comparison with reanalysis datasets. Outcomes will inform improvements to parameterizations in global climate models.

fundingcalls.title: Climate Data Fellowship 2026
fundingcalls.description: The Climate Data Fellowship 2026 supports individual researchers who use advanced data, modelling, and analytics to address climate risk, forecasting, and resilience planning. Fellows are hosted by eligible research institutions and work closely with governmental climate services and local authorities.
fundingcalls.objectives: To enhance national capacity in climate data science, improve the quality and usability of climate information services, and generate open tools and datasets that support evidence-based climate adaptation and mitigation policies.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Open to early- and mid-career researchers employed at eligible universities or public research institutes. Applicants must hold a PhD (or equivalent) by the call deadline and have an employment contract covering at least the planned fellowship period. Host institutions must be established within the country.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Applicants may request between EUR 60,000 and EUR 180,000 for a fellowship of up to 24 months. Eligible costs include personnel for the fellow, limited support staff time, equipment, data acquisition, travel, and dissemination. Travel costs, including conference participation and collaboration visits, are eligible up to a maximum of 20% of the total requested budget. Indirect costs may be claimed at a flat rate of 15% of eligible direct costs, excluding subcontracts.

fundingcalls.title: National AI Research Call 2025: Trustworthy AI in Healthcare
fundingcalls.description: This call funds collaborative research projects that develop trustworthy Artificial Intelligence methods for healthcare, including diagnosis, prognosis, treatment planning, and workflow optimisation.
fundingcalls.objectives: To advance state-of-the-art AI methods that are robust, interpretable, fair, and clinically useful; to strengthen interdisciplinary collaboration between AI researchers, clinicians, and ethicists; and to prepare AI systems for safe deployment in real-world healthcare settings.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Consortia must include at least one university or public research institute and one clinical partner. The Principal Investigator must be employed at an eligible research organisation. International partners may participate without funding, subject to institutional approval.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 300,000 and EUR 600,000 for up to 48 months. Eligible costs include personnel, equipment, data access, computing resources, travel, dissemination, and indirect costs at a maximum rate of 20% of eligible direct costs (excluding large equipment).

fundingcalls.title: Climate Adaptation Pilot Projects 2025
fundingcalls.description: The 2025 Climate Adaptation Pilots call supports pilot-scale projects that test concrete climate adaptation measures in real-world settings, such as coastal protection, flood management, and heatwave preparedness.
fundingcalls.objectives: To generate robust evidence on the effectiveness, feasibility, and social acceptance of climate adaptation measures; to support learning-by-doing in local and regional authorities; and to provide transferable models and guidelines.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Proposals must be submitted by an eligible research organisation in partnership with at least one implementing authority (e.g. municipality, regional agency). The Principal Investigator must be based at the research organisation.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 80,000 and EUR 300,000 for up to 30 months. At least 10% institutional co-funding is required, which may be in cash or in kind. Travel, equipment, and subcontracting costs are eligible when clearly linked to the pilot activities.

fundingcalls.title: Open Discovery Grants 2024
fundingcalls.description: The Open Discovery Grants 2024 call supports investigator-led basic research projects in any discipline. Proposals must be curiosity-driven and free from narrow thematic constraints.
fundingcalls.objectives: To enable excellent researchers to pursue original ideas, advance fundamental knowledge, and strengthen the national research base across all disciplines.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Individual Principal Investigators based at eligible universities or public research organisations may apply. Co-investigators and international collaborators are permitted within the budget rules.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Applicants may request between EUR 50,000 and EUR 200,000 for projects of up to 36 months. Indirect costs of up to 20% of eligible direct costs are allowed. No institutional co-funding is required.

fundingcalls.title: Early-Career Investigator Grants 2026 (Round 1)
fundingcalls.description: This call provides individual research grants for early-career researchers (0–7 years post-PhD) across all disciplines. It is designed to help promising researchers establish their own independent research agendas.
fundingcalls.objectives: To accelerate the careers of excellent early-career researchers, support the creation of independent research groups, and foster innovative, high-risk ideas that may not yet be competitive in larger schemes.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Applicants must be 0–7 years post-PhD at the call deadline, with eligible career breaks taken into account. They must hold (or have a commitment to hold) a contract at an eligible host institution covering at least the duration of the grant. PhD candidates who have not yet been awarded their degree by the project start date are not eligible to act as Principal Investigator.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Applicants may request between USD 50,000 and USD 100,000 for projects up to 36 months. Eligible costs include personnel for the PI and team members, small equipment, consumables, travel, and dissemination. Travel costs are generally expected to remain below 15% of the total requested budget. Indirect costs may be requested at up to 15% of eligible direct costs.

fundingcalls.title: Early-Career Investigator Grants 2024
fundingcalls.description: The 2024 Early-Career Investigator call supported independent projects led by researchers within seven years of their PhD across all fields of science and scholarship.
fundingcalls.objectives: To foster independence at an early career stage and support innovative lines of inquiry that strengthen the national research ecosystem.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Applicants were required to be 0–7 years post-PhD and employed at an eligible host institution. PhD students were not eligible to act as PI.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Applicants could request between EUR 75,000 and EUR 150,000 for projects of up to 36 months. Standard rules on eligible costs and indirect cost rates applied.

fundingcalls.title: Environmental Challenge Grants 2025
fundingcalls.description: The Environmental Challenge Grants 2025 call funds applied research addressing biodiversity loss, water quality, pollution reduction, and ecosystem restoration.
fundingcalls.objectives: To deliver actionable knowledge and tools that support environmental policy implementation and measurable improvements in ecological status.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Consortia led by an eligible research institution may apply, with strong involvement of stakeholder organisations such as environmental agencies, NGOs, or local authorities.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 100,000 and EUR 450,000 for up to 36 months. A minimum of 25% institutional co-funding is required. Equipment purchases above EUR 100,000 must be strongly justified.

fundingcalls.title: Sustainable Cities Initiative – 2025 Call for Proposals
fundingcalls.description: This call invites large-scale interdisciplinary projects that support sustainable urban development, low-carbon infrastructure, and climate-resilient city planning.
fundingcalls.objectives: To generate integrated solutions for sustainable mobility, energy use, housing, and green infrastructure in cities, and to promote collaboration between researchers, city administrations, and civil society.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Consortia must be led by an eligible research organisation and include at least one city or metropolitan authority as an implementation partner.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 250,000 and EUR 1,200,000 for up to 48 months. Institutional co-funding of at least 20% is required. Infrastructure investments must be directly linked to research and demonstration activities.

fundingcalls.title: National AI Research Program 2025 – Core Projects
fundingcalls.description: The core call of the National AI Research Program funds fundamental and applied AI research in areas such as machine learning, natural language processing, robotics, and AI for science and industry.
fundingcalls.objectives: To strengthen the national AI research ecosystem, promote excellence and collaboration, and support the development of trustworthy AI technologies with high scientific and societal impact.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Proposals may be submitted by eligible universities and public research institutes. Consortia are encouraged, but single-institution proposals are permitted.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 150,000 and EUR 600,000 for up to 48 months. Institutional co-funding of at least 20% is required. Personnel, equipment, data, computing, travel, and dissemination costs are eligible within program rules.

fundingcalls.title: AI and Society Challenge Grants 2024
fundingcalls.description: This call supports interdisciplinary projects examining the social, ethical, and regulatory implications of Artificial Intelligence and automated decision-making systems.
fundingcalls.objectives: To deepen understanding of how AI affects democratic institutions, labour markets, public services, and civil rights, and to generate policy-relevant recommendations for accountable AI governance.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Consortia must include both social science/humanities and technical AI expertise. The lead organisation must be an eligible research institution.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 120,000 and EUR 350,000 for up to 36 months. A minimum of 10% institutional co-funding is required. Costs for stakeholder engagement and public communication are encouraged.

fundingcalls.title: Translational Health Research Call 2025
fundingcalls.description: The 2025 call funds projects that translate basic biomedical discoveries into clinical applications, diagnostics, or interventions that improve patient outcomes.
fundingcalls.objectives: To accelerate the development of new therapies and diagnostic tools, foster collaboration between basic scientists and clinicians, and strengthen translational research capacity.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Proposals must be led by an eligible research organisation and include at least one clinical partner. Industry partners may participate under specific conditions.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 200,000 and EUR 750,000 for up to 48 months. Institutional co-funding of at least 25% is required. Clinical trial costs are eligible within defined limits.

fundingcalls.title: Social Innovation in Ageing Societies 2025
fundingcalls.description: This call funds social science and mixed-methods projects addressing ageing populations, social inclusion, and innovative care models.
fundingcalls.objectives: To inform policies and practices that support active and healthy ageing, reduce social isolation, and improve the organisation of formal and informal care.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Eligible applicants include universities and public research organisations. Partnerships with municipalities, NGOs, and care providers are strongly encouraged.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 100,000 and EUR 300,000 for up to 30 months. Institutional co-funding of at least 15% is required.

fundingcalls.title: Engineering Frontier Projects 2025
fundingcalls.description: The Engineering Frontier Projects call supports large-scale, high-risk engineering projects developing transformative technologies in energy, materials, transport, and manufacturing.
fundingcalls.objectives: To enable ambitious engineering research that has the potential to create new industries, address grand challenges, and underpin long-term economic competitiveness.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Proposals must be led by an eligible engineering department or research institute. Consortia may include industry and international partners.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: Projects may request between EUR 500,000 and EUR 1,500,000 for up to 60 months. Institutional co-funding of at least 30% is required. Major equipment purchases must be justified in relation to project objectives.

fundingcalls.title: International Collaborative Climate Research Program 2025
fundingcalls.description: A joint call with international partner agencies to support collaborative climate science, mitigation, and adaptation research involving teams from multiple countries.
fundingcalls.objectives: To promote high-impact international climate research, harmonise methodologies across countries, and support the co-production of knowledge with stakeholders.
fundingcalls.eligibilitySummary: Consortia must include eligible institutions from at least three participating countries. Each national funding agency finances its own researchers according to its rules.
fundingcalls.budgetRules: National teams may request between EUR 300,000 and EUR 900,000 for up to 48 months. Additional rules may apply at the level of participating agencies, including limits on overhead rates and equipment.
